description: Undergraduate seminar in varied aspects of biology; subject to be announced in the schedule of courses.

title: Special Topics
description: Special topics in biology.

title: Special Topics in Biology  - WR 1-4 Units
description: Topics to be indicated in the Schedule of Courses.

title: Microbes and Core Concepts in Ecology and Evolution  - WR
description: Core concepts in ecology and evolution using microbes as primary examples. Students will read primary scientific literature and write scientific reviews of the literature in order to enhance their understanding of these concepts.

title: Preview to Dentistry  - WR
description: This course is designed for pre-dental students and students considering dentistry as a career. The purpose of the course is to expose pre-dental students to the art, science, and practice of dentistry.

title: Histology
description: The microscopic anatomy and function of vertebrate tissues and organs.

title: Advanced Ecology
description: Major concepts in evolutionary ecology. Theoretical and experimental studies of adaptation, population growth and regulation, competition, predation, diversity, life histories, and ecosystems.

title: Advanced Ecology Lab
description: Field and laboratory experiments to investigate ecological principles. Discussion of research and practice of basic biostatistics and experimental design.

title: Undergraduate Research 1-3 Units
description: Undergraduate Research in a select subject area with a student-selected faculty member.

title: Undergraduate Research  - CUE 1-3 Units
description: Undergraduate Research in a select subject area with a student-selected faculty member.

title: Undergraduate Research (WR)  - CUE , WR 1-3 Units
description: Undergraduate Research in a select subject area with a student-selected faculty member.

title: Evolutionary Biology  - CUE
description: A review of basic evolutionary principles and processes including speciation, natural selection, sexual selection, extinction, heritability, genetic drift, inbreeding, outbreeding, heterochrony and Darwinian medicine.

title: Misuse of Biology in Film and Pop Culture
description: This course will examine how biology and themes in the biological sciences are portrayed in films and popular culture with a mind towards evaluating whether the science is appropriate or being misused. In the process of these analyses, we will explore such questions as: What is science and what is not? Also what is pseudo-science or bad science?

title: Biology of the Cell  - CUE , WR
description: Cellular, molecular, and biochemical aspects of cellular function.

title: Biotechnology Methods  - WR
description: Designed for Biology and Chemistry students concentrating in Genetics/Subcellular. Provides hands-on experience with basic methods used in molecular biology, including but not limited to, agarose and polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis of nucleic acids and proteins, restriction digestion of DNA, Southern and Northern hybridization, PCR, DNA sequencing, and transformation of various cell types (including bacteria, fungi, plants, insects and vertebrates).

title: Biology Seminar in Special Topics  - CUE , WR
description: Discussion of relevant research, scientific papers, and current events in biology and related natural science disciplines. Relevance and significance of the understanding of the natural principles in proper context and handling of personal and societal issues are a major focus of the discussions. This course is designed for upper division Biology majors to learn about areas of biology not currently covered elsewhere in the Biology curriculum.  This course is primarily journal-article-based and will require either oral presentations and/or written components.

title: Undergraduate Teaching Assistant  - CUE
description: A guided learning experience in inquiry-based instructional techniques and best practices in STEM education that includes a field experience as an undergraduate teaching assistant.25 GPA in major to be eligible.

title: Chemical Ecology  - WR
description: This course explores the remarkable world of chemical ecology - the role of chemicals in mediating ecological interactions within and among species. Emphasis is placed on plant chemical ecology, with additional examples from animal systems. We will examine how chemical cues influence natural and sexual selection across diverse ecological contexts. Using a discussion- and seminar-based format, students will critically analyze primary literature on chemically driven interactions, from African savannas to local ecosystems. Students will select independent research topics to develop skills in critical thinking and hypothesis generation. Discussions will integrate perspectives from genetics, molecular biology, biochemistry, natural products chemistry, ecology, and evolution. The course is intended for students with some prior knowledge of ecology and/or biochemistry.

title: Global Change Biology  - CUE
description: Human causes of global-scale changes in climate, air and water resources, and biodiversity, and impacts on people; approaches to addressing these problems that promote sustainable societies.

title: Developmental Biology  - CUE
description: Developmental biology approached from a biochemical, cellular, and anatomical perspective with emphasis on vertebrate systems.

title: Stem Cell Biology
description: This course provides an overview of different topics in stem cell biology. Students will learn about stem cell fundamentals, applications, and topics related to stem cells. In addition, students will be exposed to both primary and review literature and will engage in discussions and presentations throughout class.

title: Cancer Biology
description: Topics covered include tumor suppressors, oncogenes, cell cycle regulation, apoptosis, telomerase, angiogenesis, and metastasis. Cancer prevention, screening, diagnosis, and treatment will also be introduced.

title: Microbiology
description: A comprehensive introduction to the wide world of bacteria, viruses, and archaea and how they evolved to be so different from plants and animals. This course will delve into the basic architecture of microbial cells and their processes and discuss how microbes impact human health and disease.

title: Microbiology Laboratory
description: This lab will teach students how to work with bacteria, including human pathogens. Students will learn how to classify and identify bacteria using lab techniques and will learn about the diversity of microbes in their environment, including those found in food, on common surfaces, and natural environments. Students will also explore the

title: Advanced Human Anatomy
description: FOR BIOLOGY MAJORS ONLY.  An advanced study of the anatomical structure of the human body.  Body structure will be studied by organ systems and will involve a balance between gross anatomical study and histology.  Structure-function relationships will be emphasized.  Knowledge will be applied to clinical scenarios.

title: Principles of Physiology
description: A general study of the functions of organ systems in vertebrates.

title: Introduction to Immunology  - WR
description: Lectures will present the basic principles of immunology, with an emphasis on reading and understanding primary literature. Topics include cells and organs of the immune system, the innate and adaptive response, effector molecules, and diseases of the immune system.

title: Microbial Physiology  - CUE , WR
description: An explanation of microbial cell biology and the processes, reactions, and energetics that support microbial life in a variety of circumstances. Includes discussions of how to modify those metabolic properties to meet new demands and conditions. A general understanding of the fundamental concepts of organic chemistry is strongly recommended.

title: Biology Internship  - CUE , WR
description: Practical work experience in biological fields.

title: Independent Study 1-3 Units
description: Independent study in a selected subject area with a student-selected faculty member.

title: Independent Study 1-3 Units
description: Independent study in a selected subject area with a student-selected faculty member.

title: Independent Study  - WR 1-3 Units
description: Independent study in a selected subject area with a student-selected faculty member.

title: Behavioral Ecology
description: Evolution of behavioral adaptions for survival and reproduction; topics will include foraging, aggression, mate choice, mating strategies, and sociality.

title: Behavioral Endocrinology  - CUE , WR
description: Interactions of hormones, brain, behavior.Approved for the Arts and Sciences upper-level requirement in written communication (WR).

title: Endocrinology
description: Chemical regulation in animals.

title: Ornithology
description: Evolution, morphology, diversity, ecology, and behavior of birds. Lab stresses field identification of birds.

title: Environmental Physiology
description: Study of the physiological, morphological, behavioral and biochemical responses by eukaryotic organisms (primarily animals) to major physical and chemical factors of their environment.

title: Ichthyology
description: Introduction to anatomy, physiology, ecology, distribution, economic importance, and classification of major groups and representative local species of fish.

title: Aquatic Ecology
description: Ecological processes in aquatic environments with primary emphasis on lakes and reservoirs.

title: Metabolic Biochemistry
description: An advanced study of metabolic pathways including synthesis of macromolecules (proteins, nucleic acids, lipids, carbohydrates), mechanisms of metabolic control and utilization of metabolic intermediates.

title: Medicinal Plant Biochemistry  - CUE , WR
description: A detailed study of biosynthesis and bioactivity of specialized plant metabolites (secondary metabolites). Topics will include aspects of general plant metabolism as well as specialized metabolism pathways (alkaloids, terpenes and phenolics) and will include a survey of approaches as they relate to discovery of bioactive plant metabolites and a review of known plant metabolites used to treat human diseases/disorders.

title: Gene Structure and Function  - WR
description: Advanced topics in genetics of prokaryotes and eukaryotes, including chromosome structure and function, and gene regulation.Credit may not be earned for both BIOL 542 and BIOL 642 .

title: Experimental Design and Analysis  - CUE , WR
description: Move principles of sound experimental design, analysis and presentation from recognition to active vocabulary. Students completing the course can serve as statistical consultants for moderately complex statistical designs.

title: Evolutionary Medicine
description: In-depth analyses at the interface of evolutionary biology and the health sciences using readings from the primary literature. Format involves lectures, discussion, and a library-based research project.

title: Chronic Disease Biology
description: The course will cover the biological bases of chronic disease, with an emphasis on the mechanisms of disease causation and the evolution of these mechanisms.

title: Microbial Ecology
description: Interrelationships between microorganisms and their environments.

title: Ecology of Urban and Suburban Landscapes
description: Effects of cities and suburban sprawl on air and water chemistry, microclimate, fragmented landscapes, and responses of biotic and human communities to these conditions that degrade or promote urban resilience,adaptationn and sustainability.

title: Ecosystems Ecology
description: The transformations of matter and energy that link plant, animal and geochemical cycles. Implications for resource management also discussed.

title: Population and Community Ecology
description: Introduction to population dynamics and species interactions in aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems. Review of underlying ecological theory and its applications for conserving biodiversity.

title: Conservation Biology
description: This course provides an overview of theory and practice of conservation. Topics include biodiversity, habitat loss, the effects of habitat changes on populations, and the design and establishment of reserves. Among other things, students will learn: 1) key terms and concepts related to biodiversity, 2) significant threats to biodiversity and efforts to mitigate them, and 3) practical and socioeconomic elements of conservation biology.

title: Evolution
description: Offers a comprehensive overview of evolution and provides students with a review of issues that make up this critical discipline.

title: Selected Topics
description: Contents to be indicated in schedule of courses.

title: Selected Topics-Laboratory 1-4 Units
description: Contents to be indicated in schedule of courses.

title: Biology for Teachers I
description: Teaching pre-college biology with emphasis on curriculum content, laboratory procedures, and process skills.

title: Experimental Cell Biology for Teachers
description: Lecture and lab activities focused on inquiry-based investigations at the cellular level suitable for secondary school classroom.

title: Experimental Botany for Teachers
description: This course is designed for pre-service and classroom teachers of the life sciences from elementary through high school. To increase teachers' abilities to teach sciences, especially botany, using inquiry-oriented strategies.

title: Chemistry in Contemporary Society  - S
description: A one-semester introduction to inorganic, organic, physical, analytical, and biochemistry for non-science majors. The course is designed to provide a general background for a basic understanding of chemistry and societal issues related to chemistry, including energy, the environment, foods, materials, and the chemistry of life.

title: Introduction to General Chemistry  - S
description: A one-semester introduction to inorganic, organic, and biochemistry for Chemistry and other STEM majors as preparation for CHEM 201 for the less-prepared student with very little background in chemistry.

title: Chemistry for Health Professionals  - S
description: An introductory survey of both general and organic chemistry with an emphasis on the relationship of chemistry to life processes, health, and nutrition.

title: General Chemistry I  - S
description: An introduction to the basic concepts and principles of modern chemistry. Special emphasis on chemical periodicity, stoichiometry, equilibrium, thermodynamics, kinetics, atomic and molecular structure, and descriptive chemistry of the elements.

title: General Chemistry II  - S
description: A continuation of the basic concepts and principles of modern chemistry. Topics include equilibria, thermodynamics, kinetics, electrochemistry, nuclear chemistry and/or descriptive chemistry of the elements.

title: Introduction to Chemical Analysis I  - SL
description: An introduction to the Chemistry laboratory with an emphasis on qualitative analysis. Students are expected to have completed or be enrolled in CHEM 201 .

title: Introduction to Chemical Analysis II  - SL
description: CHEM 208 is a continuation of CHEM 207 .

title: Introduction to Chemical Analysis III
description: Continuation of CHEM 208 .

title: Introduction to Chemical Analysis IV
description: Continuation of CHEM 209 ; intended especially for Chemistry majors; introduction to instrumental methods of chemical analysis.

title: Introduction to Scientific Research
description: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of research in chemistry. Through a combination of readings, discussions, case studies, hands-on activities, and writing assignments, students will explore key topics including the scope and types of chemical research, research ethics and safety, scientific literature, the scientific method, best practices in research, and effective scientific communication. The course also provides in-depth information about on-campus research opportunities and offers guidance on how to become involved. Students will tour state-of-the-art research facilities and engage with current research initiatives through presentations by guest lecturers. In addition to active participation in lectures and in-class activities, students are required to shadow an active researcher and submit a reflective summary of the experience. Students will also write reflective pieces on one guest speaker and on the course as a whole.

title: Chemical Information Resources
description: The use of printed material and computer based literature search techniques focused on finding specific information on a designated topic.

title: Organic Chemistry I
description: Basic principles of organic chemistry: structure and geometry of molecules and consequential physical, chemical, and spectroscopic properties; chemical reactions, mechanisms, and synthetic applications; industrial and biological examples.

title: Organic Chemistry II
description: Continuation of the basic principles of organic chemistry; structure and geometry of molecules and consequential physical, chemical, and spectroscopic properties; chemical reactions, mechanism, and synthetic applications; functional group chemistry and biomolecules.

title: Organic Chemistry Laboratory I
description: Techniques of modern organic chemistry: syntheses, mechanistic studies, identification of unknowns by chemical and spectroscopic methods, special projects.

title: Organic Chemistry Laboratory II
description: Techniques of modern organic chemistry: syntheses, mechanistic studies, identification of unknowns by chemical and spectroscopic methods, special projects.

title: Undergraduate Research  - CUE , WR
description: Each student is required to submit a written report (2400 words) to the research advisor near the end of the semester. The advisor will critique the report and return it to the student for revision and submission to the advisor and the department chair at the end of the semester.

title: Undergraduate Research  - CUE 1-3 Units
description: Each student is required to submit a written report to the research advisor and the Chemistry Department chair at the end of each research period.

title: Undergraduate Research  - CUE 1-3 Units
description: Each student is required to submit a written report to the research advisor and the Chemistry Department chair at the end of each research period.

title: Cooperative Internship in Chemistry  - CUE , WR 1-3 Units
description: An individually arranged course combining work experience with related academic work in chemistry. Each student is required to submit a written report along with an employer evaluation at the end of each co-op period. The report will be reviewed and graded by the co-op director.

title: Instrumental and Statistical Analysis
description: Modern instrumental approaches to chemical analysis including atomic and molecular spectroscopy, chromatographic methods, electrochemistry, and statistical analysis of data.

title: Instrumental and Statistical Analysis Laboratory  - WR
description: Prerequisites or corequisites: CHEM 425 .  A laboratory course that covers modern instrumental approaches and advanced data analysis to chemical analysis.

title: Practicum in Chemistry Education  - CUE
description: A guided learning experience in inquiry-based instructional methods and best practices in STEM education. This course includes experimental learning as an undergraduate teaching assistant.

title: Elements of Physical Chemistry
description: Survey of Physical Chemistry. Course content includes states of matter, laws of thermodynamics and their applications, chemical and biological kinetics, quantum chemistry, and spectroscopy.

title: Survey of Biochemistry
description: A survey of chemical characteristics of the constituents of living matter; cellular metabolism of proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates and lipids.

title: Introduction to Computational Chemistry and Molecular Modeling
description: A discovery-based approach to common calculations in computational chemistry including how to optimize geometry, locate and characterize transition states, and calculate transition barriers and heats of reaction.

title: Senior Research Seminar
description: Preparation of the Senior Thesis and an oral presentation of it before a group of peers and faculty.

title: Physical Chemistry I
description: Introduction to the quantum theory of atoms, molecules and the chemical bond. The use of spectroscopy in the determination of molecular structure and chemical function.

title: Physical Chemistry II
description: Thermodynamics, statistical thermodynamics, chemical eqilibrium and reaction kinetics from a molecular point of view.

title: Physical Chemistry Laboratory  - WR
description: Laboratory experiments and related lecture on properties of gases, thermochemistry, kinetics, electro-chemistry, laser techniques in chemical processes, spectroscopic methods including FTIR, EPR and X-ray crystallography.

title: Undergraduate Research  - CUE 1-3 Units
description: Each student is required to submit a written report to the research advisor and the Chemistry Department chair at the end of each research period.

title: Undergraduate Research 1-3 Units
description: Each student is required to submit a written report to the research advisor and the Chemistry Department chair at the end of each research period.

title: Independent Study 1-3 Units
description: Independent research conducted with the approval and supervision of a faculty member.

title: Special Topics in Chemistry 1-3 Units
description: An examination of one or more specified areas of Chemistry. Details announced each semester.

title: Inorganic Chemistry
description: Descriptive and theoretical chemistry of the elements.

title: Spectroscopic Identification of Organic Compounds  - WR
description: An introductory spectroscopy course designed to provide a realistic experience in organic structure determination using the principal methods of spectral characterization. Emphasis is placed on using mass spectrometry, 1H and 13C nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, and infrared spectroscopy to deduce the molecular structures of organic compounds.

title: Contemporary Methods of Organic Synthesis and Analysis  - CUE
description: A discovery-based approach to the solution of problems encountered in organic chemical synthesis and analysis.

title: Contemporary Methods of Inorganic Synthesis and Analysis  - WR
description: Prerequisites or corequisites: CHEM 515 .  A discovery-based approach to the solution of problems encountered in inorganic chemical synthesis and analysis.

title: Chemistry Education for Secondary Teachers
description: Inquiry-based laboratory and diverse assessment of core concepts will be developed.

title: Biochemistry I
description: Chemistry of amino acids, peptides, proteins,  carbohydrates, nucleotides and nucleic acids; methods of analysis and laboratory synthesis; enzyme properties, kinetics, ligand binding.

title: Biochemistry Laboratory
description: Application of contemporary laboratory methods of biochemistry including uv/vis spectrophotometry, liquid chromatography centrifugation, gel electrophoresis, mass spectrometry, FTIR, and recombinant DNA techniques.

title: Biochemistry II
description: Cellular metabolism of carbohydrates, lipids, amino acids and biomembrane phenomena; RNA, DNA, and protein synthesis.

title: Group Theory and its Chemical Applications
Theory and Application of Computational Chemistry
description: Theory and Application of Computational Chemistry will be a combined elective undergraduate and graduate level course. The purpose of the course is to provide students with the necessary knowledge such that they can examine chemical problems computationally and where required, use computational tools to benefit their own research. The course will emphasize practical applications of computers in chemistry, trends that define the appropriate methods for chemical studies, and elucidation of underlying theoretical methods. As the abstract nature of the field is frequently a learning barrier to many students, the course will incorporate a series of workshops to enable students to build their own computer programs and study chemical problems computationally.

title: Bio-Organic Phenomena
description: Completion of CHEM 445 , CHEM 545 , or equivalent is strongly recommended.  Special topics in the biological chemistry area: e.g., chemical carcinogenesis; diet and cancer, food chemistry and polypeptides; proteins; carbohydrates; enzymes; hormone chemistry.

title: Chemistry for Teachers I
description: Teaching pre-college chemistry with emphasis on curriculum content, laboratory procedures, and process skills.

title: Tales of the Chinese Diaspora  - AH , P2
description: An introduction to Chinese historical, global migrations in modern times, the reciprocal influences between Chinese and non-Chinese cultures, and Chinese ethnicity and identities within the global context.

title: Chinese Popular Culture  - AH , P2
description: This course is an introduction to modern and contemporary Chinese pop culture and its global significance.

title: Special Topics
description: Topic varies by semester. Conducted in English.

title: Doing Business in China
description: Discussion of business traditions, practices, etiquette, and norms in China. Conducted in English.

title: Chinese Culture and Tradition
description: Discussion of Chinese culture and society from the beginnings to 1900, covering topics of religion and government, education, marriage, family, medicine, invention, art, architecture, and other aspects of social life. Conducted in English.

title: Contemporary China
description: Discussion of the China of today, including popular culture, technology, mass media, folk theater, urban consumer culture, and state policy. Conducted in English.

title: China Through Film  - AH , P2
description: Examination of 20th-century China through the critical study of selected examples of film. Conducted in English.

title: Ethnicity and Religion in China
description: Studies of minority peoples such as Uigurs, Huis, Mongols and Tibetans, including their life style, language, art and religion. Discussion of how Buddhism, Islam, Christianity and other religious ideas spread into China, and their presence today in China. Conducted in English.

title: Chinese Literature in Translation I  - AH , P2
description: Chinese literature from the beginning to 1911, discussion of principal genres, authors, and works. Conducted in English.

title: Chinese Literary Theory
description: Discussion of Chinese views of the function of poetry and literature, ancient Chinese aesthetics, and modern literary politics. Conducted in English.

title: Western Literature in China
description: Discussion of the tradition of Chinese translation of foreign authors beginning in the late 19th century and its impact on Chinese literature, culture and society, with Katherine Mansfield, Mark Twain, Ernest Hemingway, James Joyce and others as examples. Conducted in English.

title: Mechanics I: Statics
description: Apply fundamental concepts of statics to examine forces, equilibrium, friction, centroids and moments of inertia, to analyze and solve engineering problems. Both vector and scalar methodologies are used.

title: Mechanics of Solids
description: Analysis of deformable engineered members subjected to concentric, flexural, torsion, and combined loading. Examination of statically determinate and indeterminate systems to analyze and design engineering structures.

title: Mechanics of Materials Laboratory
description: A materials testing laboratory in which the properties of materials such as wood and steel are evaluated. Performances of tension, compression, shear, bending, and torsion tests on various specimens. Experimental determination of stresses. CEE majors.

title: Civil Engineering Field Measurements
description: Focus is on plane surveying, including the use and care of surveying instruments for laying out engineered facilities. Use of proprietary Computer-Aided Engineering software is introduced.

title: Civil Engineering Field Measurements Laboratory
description: Practical applications of plane surveying techniques, including the use and care of surveying instruments, and laying out engineered facilities are introduced.

title: Civil and Environmental Engineering Cooperative Education Seminar
description: Discussion of the policies and procedures for cooperative education and instruction in self-directed job search techniques, including interviewing skills, resume preparation, and guidelines for the co-op report. This is a prerequisite for each cooperative education term.

title: Civil and Environmental Engineering Cooperative Education I
description: Full-time technical work experience related to the student's academic program.

title: Introduction to Environmental Engineering
description: This course is intended as an introduction to the basic science and engineering principles that are involved in assessment of environmental quality, in understanding environmental processes and in developing solutions to environmental problems.

title: Structural Analysis
description: Classical approaches to analysis determinate and indeterminate structures, including trusses, beams, and frames are presented. Different methods are used to determine the internal forces and the deformation of these structural systems.

title: Engineering Hydraulics
description: A study of the fundamental principles of hydraulics, including fluid statics, kinematics, and dynamics. Application of basic concepts and principles to fluid flow through pipes, open channels, turbo machines, and flow measurement.

title: Engineering Hydraulics Lab
description: A hydraulic experimental laboratory in which the static and dynamic properties of fluids are evaluated. Experimental determination of fluid properties, fluid behavior, and the flow dynamics including flowrates and forces.

title: Civil and Environmental Engineering Cooperative Education II
description: Full-time technical work experience related to the student's academic program.

title: Civil Engineering Professional Practice
description: Professional practices, issues relating to effective communication skills (written and oral) and engineering ethics are emphasized. The course includes  simulations and presentations by legal and engineering practitioners.

title: Practicum in Civil and Environmental Engineering Education
description: A guided learning experience in inquiry-based instructional techniques and best practices in STEM education that includes a field experience as an undergraduate teaching assistant. Permission to enroll required.

title: Fundamentals of Concrete Design
description: Introduction to concrete design specifications (ACI); the design of structural systems in concrete, slabs, buildings, and frames.

title: Fundamentals of Steel Design
description: Introduction to the AISC LRFD steel design specification. Design of steel tension members, beams, columns, beam-columns, and connections.

title: Geomechanics
description: Application of mechanical principles to the behavior of soil masses. Soil mineralogy; hydraulic behavior; consolidation theory and settlement analyses; shear strength.

title: Geomechanics Laboratory
description: Laboratory measurement of soil properties including index properties, permeability, compressibility, and sheer strength.

title: Foundation Engineering
description: Character of natural soil deposits. Subsurface exploration and testing. Foundation types, limitations. Bearing capacity and settlement analyses. Design of foundations.

title: Transportation Systems Engineering
description: A study of the planning, design, implementation, and evaluation of transportation systems across the modes. Issues of legislation, regulation, and funding are also examined.

title: Surface Water Hydrology
description: Hydrologic cycle, rainfall, rainfall abstractions including infiltration theory, and runoff. Unit hydrograph theory and its application in runoff computation from watersheds. Statistical applications in hydrology including frequency analysis, flood routing and hydrologic engineering and design.

title: Water Supply and Sewerage
description: Quantity of water and sewage; collection and distribution of water supplies; quality of water supplies; characteristics of sewage and disposal; principles and design of processes for preliminary, primary and secondary treatment of water supplies and sewage including screening, clarification and filtration; and stormwater management.

title: Civil & Environmental Engineering Capstone Design  - CUE
description: An introduction to integrated civil engineering design, incorporating aspects of geotechnical, structural, transportation, and water resources engineering into a more comprehensive design project.

title: Civil Engineering Cooperative Education III
description: Full-time technical work experience related to the student's academic program.

title: Fundamentals of Engineering Exam Review
description: Review of topics covered on eight-hour NCEES Fundamentals of Engineering  examination. Not to be counted towards meeting the requirements for a degree.

title: Design of Structural Systems
description: Introduction to the design of structural systems. Model building codes. Material, structural system and foundation system selection. Design of connections. Economic, detailing constructability and erection issues. Preparation of engineering drawings. Review of shop drawing.

title: Fundamentals of Prestressed Concrete
description: Introduction to pre-tensioned and post-tensioned prestressed concrete. Design of precast concrete slabs, buildings, and bridges in accordance with ACI specifications and the Prestressed Concrete Institute (PCI) recommended practices. Application of computer programs for member analysis and design.

title: Timber Design
description: Concepts of Structural Timber design will be taught. The properties of wood materials will be reviewed and the procedures for the design of typical timber components will be presented. In addition to course assignments, and tests, required of all students, students taking this course for graduate credit will be required to complete a group design of a simple building.

title: Bridge Design
description: This class offers detailed coverage of engineering basics for the design of short- and medium-span bridges.

title: Construction Materials
description: Properties of construction materials such as cement, concrete, asphalt, and structural elastomers. Design of Portland cement concrete and asphaltic concrete mixes.

title: Experimental Stress Analysis
description: Fundamentals of experimental stress analysis, brittle coating, photoelastic coating, and electrical strain gage techniques, strain measurements under static and dynamic loading.

title: Construction Management
description: This course discusses construction management including estimating, bidding, time/cost management, provisions, surety bonds, ownership, and project scheduling. Other topics, such as temporary erosion control and plan & print reading will also be covered.

title: Construction Equipment and Practice
description: This course will provide an overview of construction planning and practices rather than construction management. The emphasis will be on the Construction Depth content of Civil Engineering. The topics covered in the course will be guided by the anticipated content of fundamental construction engineering practice and will follow the various chapters of the required textbook.

title: Earth Pressures and Retaining Structures
description: Earth pressure calculation: theory and practice. Design techniques for retaining walls, reinforced earth and soil nailing.

title: Advanced Foundation Design
description: This course covers site investigation and subsurface exploration with the purpose of foundation design. Then, the course covers the analysis and design of single piles and group piles considering pile capacity and pile head displacement. At the end, the course covers pile driving analysis to test the integrity of the concrete.

title: Traffic Engineering
description: Examines characteristics of the vehicle, the driver, and the traffic stream. Highway and intersection capacity, theory of traffic flow, parking, traffic safety.

title: Geometric Design of Highways
description: The concepts of geometric design for rural and urban highways, utilizing proprietary design software are introduced and applied.

title: GPS Theory and Application
description: This course is designed to give the student an overview of the use of Global Positioning Systems in surveying and engineering applications. Elements of coordinate systems ,map projections, GPS principles of operation, mapping, boundary, and construction surveys will be covered.

title: GIS  Applications to Transportation
description: Introduces an overview and application of current methods of implementing GIS solutions to highway data analysis and planning studies.

title: Applied Hydraulics
description: Application of basic principles of hydraulic engineering to analysis of flow in floodways, through bridge openings, culverts, and spillways. Analysis of stable channel design is also considered. Commonly used computer programs are utilized to design structures in floodways.

title: Applied Hydrology
description: Introduction to hydrologic systems; modeling runoff from watersheds using lumped and distributed methods; stormwater management and design; hydrologic and hydraulic routing including kinematic wave routing; computer rainfall-runoff simulation models. A hydrologic design project will be assigned to all students; special assignments dealing with hydrologic processes will be assigned to MS degree students.

title: Open Channel Hydraulics
description: Application of basic principles of hydraulics to open channel flow. Theory and analysis of critical, uniform and gradually varied flow and computer analysis. Select topics in rapidly varied and unsteady flow.

title: Groundwater Hydrology
description: Fundamental concepts of fluid flow and soil properties; theory of groundwater movement; mechanics of well flow; groundwater contaminant transport.

title: Current Topics in Civil Engineering 1-4 Units
description: An examination of one or more topics in Civil Engineering. Details announced each semester.

title: Introduction to Criminal Justice  - SB
description: Overview of crime and the response of the United States justice system, focusing on law enforcement, courts, and corrections. Explores the impact of crime on communities and examines how the justice system responds to these issues in contemporary society.

title: Introduction to Law Enforcement  - SB
description: Introduction to the development of law enforcement in the United States, including its history, models, organizations, and current challenges.

title: Introduction to Corrections  - SB
description: Introductory examination of the history, practices, and challenges in corrections, focusing on institutional and community corrections systems in the United States.

title: Introduction to Law and the Legal System
description: This course will introduce you to the various legal fields and specialties.  Legal aspects of current topics will be discussed to assist you in acquiring knowledge and appreciation of the law in our changing society.  Legal Vocabulary will be emphasized.  This course will introduce you to an overview of the legal profession (to include attorneys, judges, paralegals, law enforcement and professions that have significant contact with the judicial system).

title: Criminal Justice Occupational Courses and Learning 1-24 Units
description: The student does not register for this course. It is a receptacle course to award PLA credit upon completion of our PLA course CJ 304 .

title: Career Development and Cultural Intelligence  - P1 , SB
description: Examines career development in criminal justice by fostering awareness of cultural diversity, also known as cultural intelligence.

title: Introduction to Digital Forensics and Media Analysis
description: Introduction to the collection, preservation, and presentation of computer-based evidence for criminal law enforcement and civil litigation. Covers the role of computer forensic practitioners in investigating digital crime scenes and related legal issues.

title: Criminal Justice Prior Learning Assessment (PLA) 1-2 Units
description: Equip students with skills to document and assess prior learning from occupational training. Students create a Prior Learning Assessment Document (PLAD) that combines reflection and documentation for evaluation and college credit. Writing, thinking, and technology skills are emphasized.

title: Theories of Crime and Behavior
description: Explores theories and research on criminality from biological, psychological, and sociological perspectives.

title: Criminal Procedure
description: Examines constitutional aspects of criminal procedure, focusing on investigation, detention, prosecution, and adjudication, including due process and the exclusionary rule.

title: Criminal Justice Research Methods
description: Introduction to social research methods in criminal justice, emphasizing both quantitative and qualitative approaches.

title: Quantitative Analysis in Criminal Justice  - QR
description: Other course readiness markers for this class are SAT (510) or completion of GEN 103 .  Introduction to statistics and analysis of criminal justice data, emphasizing measurement, descriptive, and inferential statistical methods in criminal justice research.

title: Court Administration
description: Studies court organization, administration, and management through the lens of a court administrator. Focuses on the development of court systems, efficient courtroom and case flow management, and operations of trial and appellate courts.

title: Sex Crimes
description: Interdisciplinary exploration of sex offenses and crimes related to sexual conduct. Topics cover sexual deviancy, client profiles and treatment, and the role of societal values and institutions in shaping sexual behavior and norms.

title: Crime and the Media
description: Examines how crime is represented in mass media and how these portrayals influence public perceptions of crime and the justice system. Explores the social construction of crime through media narratives and their impact on justice policies and practices.

title: Forensic Science and Investigative Techniques
description: Survey of techniques and procedures in scientific crime investigation. Covers the history of forensic investigation, research methods, data analysis, and the role of scientific expertise in the prosecutorial process.

title: Harm Reduction and Public Safety
description: Explores criminological and environmental theories and methods for crime prevention and reduction. Covers the history of prevention strategies, program development, case studies, research, and loss-prevention management in the United States.

title: Private Security Management
description: Study of management functions within a private security agency, including structure, personnel, legal status, budgeting, and supervision.

title: Juvenile Justice
description: Explores the theories and philosophies influencing the evolution of the juvenile justice system in the United States. Examines history, key issues, and the roles of law enforcement, courts, corrections, and community in its contemporary model.

title: Artificial Intelligence in Criminal Justice
description: This course introduces students to generative artificial intelligence (AI) and its implications for society through a criminal justice lens.  Students will explore the historical foundations of AI, the evolving role of humans in guiding AI systems, and practical approaches to interacting with AI through prompt design.  The emphasis is placed on building critical understanding alongside hands on engagement with AI tools.

title: Artificial Intelligence Issues in Criminal Justice
description: This course provides students with a user-oriented perspective of the major issues concerning criminal justice and artificial intelligence.  The course will cover a myriad of topics including but not limited to risk assessment, predictive policing, ethical oversight, and criminal justice policy implications.

title: Law, Ethics and Artificial Intelligence
description: This course explores the intersection of the algorithmic systems of law and artificial intelligence (AI), focusing on the legal implications, challenges, and opportunities presented by the rapid advancements in AI technologies. Students will examine the legal frameworks governing AI, ethical considerations with AI, liability issues, intellectual property concerns, and the role of AI in shaping various social fields such as criminal justice, intellectual property, privacy, and employment law.

title: Community Corrections
description: Explores the development of community-based correctional programs beyond traditional institutions, including diversion, probation, parole, home incarceration, electronic monitoring, and other initiatives for individuals involved in the justice system.

title: Introduction to Cyber Investigations
description: Explores tools for network monitoring, traffic collection, and digital investigations, as well as data processing and forensic technologies in online and digital environments. Covers advanced topics in network security and digital forensics.

title: Law Enforcement Administration
description: Examines police organization and administration, covering the history of law enforcement structures, the political environment of agencies, the role of law enforcement in different societies, and key management functions.

title: Restorative Justice
description: Links criminology with broader social contexts while considering the causes of harm. Explores assumptions about crime and justice through retributive and restorative frameworks, emphasizing accountability, repair, and fairness in justice practices.

title: Criminal Court Procedures
description: Examines criminal due process and legal procedures from apprehension to sentencing. Covers key aspects of the criminal justice process, including disposition, bail, extradition, hearings, trial, and post-sentencing procedures like probation and parole.

title: Criminal Law and Evidence
description: Examines criminal liability and the processes of criminalization, investigation, and prosecution, covering various offenses and defenses while emphasizing state and federal evidence rules.

title: Criminal Justice Advanced Occupational Courses and Learning 1-18 Units
description: The student does not register for this course. It is a receptacle course to award PLA credit upon completion of our PLA course CJ 304 .

title: Legal Issues in Corrections
description: Overview of legal issues related to the constitutional rights of individuals serving their sentences in the community and correctional facilities.

title: Institutional Corrections
description: Examines the design, management, and operation of jails and prisons in the United States, including the experiences of those incarcerated.

title: Crimes Against Older Adults
description: Examines federal and state laws designed to protect older adults, focusing on legal protections and the criminal justice system's response to the mistreatment and exploitation of this population.

title: Crime Victims and Victimization
description: Overview of crime victimization and the criminal justice system response to victims and survivors.

title: Drugs and Crime
description: Explores the history, causes, and extent of substance use disorders, focusing on their connection to harmful or unlawful behavior and the role of criminal justice agencies.

title: Computer Applications in Criminal Justice
description: Applied introduction to computer applications in criminal justice and criminology. Covers data collection, basic analysis, electronic research methods, and web page design, with an emphasis on the use of software and hardware in criminal justice agencies.

title: Organized Crime
description: Interdisciplinary exploration of key issues related to organized crime. Examines historical contexts, theoretical frameworks, and various criminal activities associated with organized crime groups.

title: Behavioral Analysis of Violent Crime
description: Examines the assessment of fatal violence, covering topics such as organized and disorganized behavioral patterns, stages of homicide, blood spatter analysis, investigative processes, media influence, and typologies of individuals involved in violent crimes.

title: Violence in the United States
description: Explores various forms of interpersonal violence, including intimate partner violence, child abuse, abuse of older adults, sexual violence, assault, and workplace violence. Emphasizes the criminal justice system's role in responding to and addressing these issues.

title: Environmental Crime
description: Explores theoretical and practical legal issues in environmental criminal justice, with an introduction to criminological approaches to addressing environmental problems.

title: Comparative Criminal Justice  - WR
description: Overview of international criminal justice systems and the key issues that arise within them across different countries.

title: Human Trafficking
description: Examines the trafficking of human beings in its historical, legal, economic, political, and social contexts.

title: Computer and Electronic  Crime
description: Examines legal policy and technical issues related to computer and electronic crime.

title: Internship 3-6 Units
description: Offers hands-on learning opportunities that highlight the application of theoretical knowledge in real-world contexts within criminal justice and related fields.

title: Correctional Rehabilitation
description: Examines techniques and methods used by the correctional system to influence and change client behavior. Topics include the history of treatment, current programs, studies on effectiveness, and the future of rehabilitation in the United States.

title: Police Administration
description: A discussion of police administration, organization, and functions. Administration is approached from the points of view of a police executive and his or her principal aides. Emphasis encompasses both reality and theory. Contemporary management thought is emphasized.

title: Law and Police Administration
description: Provides a detailed analysis of legal issues in police administration and management, with an emphasis on developing policies, procedures, and practices to mitigate liability in administrative operations. Case studies highlight emerging trends and current issues.

title: Police Organizational Behavior
description: Examines basic principles of human behavior with a focus on how they are applied to police work in organizational settings.

title: Criminal Justice Senior Seminar  - CUE , WR
description: Senior level seminar that explores and discusses the challenges and issues in the administration and management of criminal justice organizations.

title: Police and the Community
description: Examines the relationship between communities and law enforcement, covering the history of policing, police community relations, and Community Oriented Policing.

title: Directed Studies 1-9 Units
description: Directed readings or research centered on specialized interests in criminal justice issues and topics.

title: Special Topics in Criminal Justice 1-9 Units
description: Covers specialized topics in criminal justice.

title: Criminal Justice Honors Thesis  - WR
description: Intensive examination of a topical area in criminal justice. Students work individually with designated faculty advisor to develop and complete original research projects.

title: Crime and Criminal Justice in Film
description: Examines media representations of the criminal justice system within a historical context and global perspective, highlighting the congruence and disparities between popular film and reality.

title: International Terrorism
description: Explores international terrorism and terrorist organizations, including their causes, operations, funding, and future threats. Covers biological, chemical, nuclear, and cyber terrorism, as well as the media's influence on terrorism and counter-terrorism efforts.

title: Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design
description: Introduces Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design (CPTED). Emphasizes opportunity theories of crime and their application, and explores strategies for preventing crime by modifying the built environment.

title: Crime Analysis
description: Covers introductory techniques of crime analysis and mapping, focusing on applying theoretical concepts to crime data.

title: Managing Conflict, Change, and Justice
description: Explores the interpersonal and social aspects of conflict and essential practices for conflict resolution. Analyzes human dynamics in conflicts, emphasizing the significance of conflict management in building relationships.

title: Seminar in Juvenile Justice Issues
description: An interdisciplinary seminar examining conflicting perspectives within the juvenile justice system and related issues. Topics include an overview of the juvenile justice system, treatment and prevention programs, unique laws affecting juveniles, and other pertinent themes.

title: Gangs in the United States
description: Examines gangs, including their history, definitions, and the criminal justice system's response. Covers theories of gang involvement, current trends, and prevention and intervention strategies, offering an overview of the complexities of gang activity and its impact on communities and criminal justice policy.

title: Domestic and Family Violence
description: Explores the dynamics of family violence, focusing on intimate partner violence, child maltreatment, and abuse of older adults across diverse family structures and life stages.

title: Capital Punishment  - WR
description: Explores issues related to the death penalty, including constitutional questions, the impact of death row incarceration, and public attitudes, with a special focus on research findings from Kentucky.

title: Corporate and Financial Crime  - WR
description: Explores corporate and financial crime, focusing on key concepts, theoretical explanations, and the extent and nature of these offenses across various sectors, including both public and private organizations.

title: Serial and Mass Murder
description: Examines serial and mass murder in the United States, focusing on the relationship between these crimes and factors such as race, gender, media portrayal, and behavioral analysis. Explores the impact of mass murder on communities, including political and religious ideologies, affected institutions, and the responses from law enforcement, community members, and policymakers.

title: Race and Gender in Criminal Justice  - WR
description: Examines the dynamics of racial discrimination and disparity in the United States and their impact on the criminal justice system.

title: Introduction to Homeland Security
description: Outlines the philosophy, purpose, and rationale for a dedicated government agency focused on domestic security. Explores issues related to international and domestic terrorism and their effects on homeland security trends.

title: Managing Organizational Performance
description: Examination of factors affecting the management of organizational performance. Designed to help participants develop conceptual diagnostic and problem solving skills required of strategic managers in contemporary law enforcement organizations.

title: Trauma Informed Policing
description: Trauma informed policing focuses on how law enforcement can improve police community interactions, service to the public, and internal operations through adopting a trauma informed approach. This course will discuss evidence-based methods to recognize, understand, and appropriately respond to trauma experienced by victims, offenders, and police officers.

title: Criminal Justice Leadership  - WR
description: Through the use of lectures and case studies (films), participants will be provided with a cognitive understanding of leadership that emphasizes the study of leadership models and theories. Study and evaluation of various leadership styles and processes.

title: International Service Learning: Criminal Justice Seminar
description: Study of criminology and criminal justice with a cross cultural perspective. Comparative criminal justice research regarding particular problems and issues in the host country will be conducted and examined.

title: Seminar in Criminal Justice
description: Explores selected topics in Criminal Justice.

title: Ethics in Criminal Justice  - WR
description: Studies ethics and ethical decision-making in the criminal justice system.

title: Criminal Justice Behavior Dynamics
description: A study of the basic principles of human behavior with emphasis on the application of these principles to police work in organizational settings.  Consideration is given to the dynamics of both individual and group behavior in working environments.

title: Dental Hygiene Academic Orientation
description: This course is designed especially for dental hygiene students. It will include understanding a career in dental hygiene, academic policies and requirements, and making use of the diverse resources offered at a metropolitan research university.

title: Dental Hygiene Theory I
description: Provides the fundamentals of scientific knowledge and clinical skills necessary for entering the clinical phase of the curriculum of dental hygiene.

title: Dental Hygiene Principles of Practice I
description: This course is a laboratory course teaching students the concepts and principles of dental instrumentation.

title: Dental Anatomy
description: Nomenclature, morphology, structure, function and occlusion of teeth.

title: Radiography
description: History and development of the x-ray, its nature and properties, safety precautions, and uses of the x-ray in dentistry. Theory in the fundamentals of oral radiographic technique, darkroom processing, and dental radiographic analysis.

title: Structural Biology of Head and Neck
description: Origin and structure of tissues, histology and embryology of teeth, face and oral cavity. Biochemistry as it relates to oral structures. Study of anatomy of oral structures.

title: Physical Diagnosis and Medical Management
description: Course teaches clinical patient evaluation, emphasizing medical histories and vital signs. Students learn to recognize and manage patients who need treatment and modifications or special precautions for safe dental care.

title: Pre-Clinical Radiography
description: Laboratory experiences in film placement, film mounting, exposure and processing techniques. Application of the techniques of radiographic surveys.

title: General and Oral Pathology
description: Study of disease mechanisms affecting the human body, with particular emphasis on diseases of the oral and paraoral structures. Clinical diagnostic criteria are emphasized and correlated with pathological entities.

title: Periodontology
description: An understanding of the etiology, mechanisms and processes involved in periodontal disease. Emphasis on integration of research findings to provide evidence-based periodontal therapy.

title: Dental Materials
description: Physical properties of dental materials and basic principles of their preparation.Students work on typodonts, placing rubber dams, applying matrices, inserting bases and restorative material in anterior and posterior prepared teeth. Polishing of restorations and taking impressions for diagnostic study models are introduced.

title: Oral Health Education
description: Course addresses theories and principles of health education, assessment and evaluation techniques, and  teaching strategies with regards to oral health education. Will include information specific to selected topics such as tobacco cessation and community health education.

title: Dental Hygiene Theory II
description: This course is a continuation of DHED 301 .

title: Dental Hygiene Principles of Practice II
description: This course is the laboratory continuation and beginning clinical component of DHED 302 .

title: Pain and Anxiety Control
description: Provides basic understanding in the use of local anesthesia and anxiety management techniques for the control of pain and anxiety in the clinical practice of dental hygiene.

title: Community Dental Health I
description: Course provides students with an overview of the dental health care system, introduces students to epidemiological study methodologies, explores selected dental public health practices and introduces basic skills in program planning.

title: Pharmacology
description: Course focus is drugs and anesthetics, with emphasis on those used in the dental office. Discussion of the origin of drugs and anesthetics, physical and chemical properties, preparation, mode of administration and effects on body systems.

title: Evidenced Based Practice
description: This course is designed to provide students with the analytical skills needed to interpret and evaluate studies that include the collection and analysis of data. The course focuses on research design, and descriptive and inferential techniques commonly used research activities.

title: Special Needs Patients
description: Course teaches students about the management of dental patients with a variety of physical, emotional, and psychosocial needs. Will include information about the demographic and sociological trends concerning the elderly  and will relate those trends to the dental care delivery system.

title: Advanced Periodontology I
description: Introduction to the use of chemotherapeutics in the treatment of periodontal disease. Use of case studies to enhance recognition of periodontal disease and identification of subsequent treatment.

title: Community Dental Health II
description: Course focuses on skills development in assessment, planning, implementation, and evaluation of dental public health programs. Will include practicum to enhance student experience in program planning and critical thinking skills

title: Advanced Technology and Patient Care Procedures I
description: Correlation of medical conditions to dental hygiene treatment utilizing both lecture and case study seminars. Emphasizes the application of information technology to improve the management of diagnostic data.

title: Dental Hygiene Principles of Practice III
description: Continuation of DHED 314 including rotations to dental clinics in other facilities to further acquaint students with diverse oral and medical conditions.

title: Clinical Radiography I
description: Clinical application of radiography and radiology.

title: Advanced Radiology
description: This course teaches advanced extra oral radiography, advanced interpretation and introduction into radiographic case studies. Some campus visits are required to complete the course requirements.

title: Clinical Radiography II
description: Clinical application of radiography and radiology. A continuation of DHED 409 .

title: Dental Hygiene Principles of Practice IV
description: Continuation of DHED 408 .

title: Advanced Periodontology II
description: Continuation of DHED 405 .

title: Advanced Technology and Patient Care Procedures II
description: Continuation of DHED 407 .

title: Dental Hygiene Ethics and Practice Mangement
description: Role of the dental hygienist as related to the health professions and to the dental hygiene association; relationship of the dental hygienist to members of the health team; principles of professional ethics, laws, rules and regulations covering the practice of dental hygiene and dentistry; introduction to the administration and management of a dental health team.

title: Dental Hygiene Extramural Education  - CUE
description: Extramural clinical rotation that affords the student an opportunity to provide patient care and community service projects within a health care delivery system.

title: Foundation 2-D Design
description: Exploration of the principles and elements of two-dimensional design through the study of line, form, value, color, and composition. Emphasis is placed on visual hierarchy, rhythm, and clarity as ideas are developed into cohesive visual statements. Projects integrate abstract and communicative approaches using type, image, and other design elements to build a strong foundation in visual organization and design thinking.

title: Foundation 3-D Design
description: Exploration of the principles and elements of three-dimensional design through the study of line, form, space, light, color, and composition. Emphasis is placed on proportion, structure, and the behavior of materials through hands-on making. Projects develop spatial awareness and an understanding of how form and material communicate in three dimensions.

title: Foundation Drawing
description: Introduction to the principles and practices of drawing as a means of seeing, thinking, and visual communication. Focuses on line, value, mark-making, structure, and composition through observational and conceptual studies. Emphasis is placed on developing perceptual accuracy, spatial understanding, and the use of drawing to support ideation and design processes.

title: Lab: InDesign
description: Practice in page layout and typographic systems. Focus on master pages, styles, preflight, and exports suited to print and screen.Passing the exam earns an industry-recognized certificate and a digital badge, which can be showcased on portfolios, resumes, websites, LinkedIn, and email signatures. This course is required for completion of the BFA tracks in Graphic Design, Interaction Design, Interior Design, and Product Design.

title: Lab: Photoshop
description: Image editing and compositing with nondestructive workflows. Attention to narrative clarity, accessibility, and platform requirements.Passing the exam earns an industry-recognized certificate and a digital badge, which can be showcased on portfolios, resumes, websites, LinkedIn, and email signatures. This course is required for completion of the BFA tracks in Graphic Design, Interaction Design, Interior Design, and Product Design.

title: Lab: Illustrator
description: Vector drawing for icons, symbols, and scalable graphics. Projects emphasize precision, consistency, and cross-media delivery.Passing the exam earns an industry-recognized certificate and a digital badge, which can be showcased on portfolios, resumes, websites, LinkedIn, and email signatures. This course is required for completion of the BFA tracks in Graphic Design, Interaction Design, Interior Design, and Product Design.

title: Lab: After Effects
description: Introduction to motion principles, keyframing, and render workflows. Emphasis on type-in-motion and clarity of timing and pacing.Passing the exam earns an industry-recognized certificate and a digital badge, which can be showcased on portfolios, resumes, websites, LinkedIn, and email signatures. This course is required for completion of the BFA tracks in Graphic Design, Interaction Design, Interior Design, and Product Design.

title: Principles of Graphic Design
description: Introduction to design methods, research, ideation, iteration, and critique applied to communication challenges across media.This course is required for completion of the BFA tracks in Graphic Design, Interaction Design, Interior Design, and Product Design.

title: Foundation Interior Design
description: An introduction to the discourse of design and the process of design through methods of representation and making. This course is required for students who intend to apply for the BFA program in Interior Design and must be taken concurrently with DES 361 or ART 361 .

title: Foundation Graphic Design
description: Fundamental methods of solving visual communication problems found in the design arts with an emphasis on clarity and immediacy of the solution for a general audience. This course is required for students who intend to apply for the BFA program in Graphic Design.

title: History of Graphic Design
description: Seminar covering the evolution of graphic design from 1800 to the present, highlighting major movements, technologies, ethical considerations, and critical theory. History & Theory.This course is required for completion of the BFA tracks in Graphic Design, Interaction Design, Interior Design, and Product Design.

title: Lab: Maker Spaces
description: Provides hands-on experience in digital fabrication and prototyping, utilizing maker-space tools such as laser cutters, 3D printers, and CNC equipment.

title: Human Experience in Design
description: Applies human-centered design methods through iterative research, ideation, prototyping, and testing within a collaborative lab environment.This course is required for completion of the BFA tracks in Graphic Design, Interaction Design, Interior Design, and Product Design.

title: Special Topics in Design 1.5-3 Units
description: Explores current and emerging topics in design through focused seminars or studio projects. Content varies by offering.This course is an elective for the BFA tracks in Graphic Design, Interaction Design, Interior Design, and Product Design.

title: Principles of Typography
description: Introduces typefaces, anatomy, measurement, and typographic structures to develop clear, effective typographic communication.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Graphic Design.

title: Information Design
description: Focus on organizing complex information into clear, accessible visual narratives through diagrams, charts, and data visualization.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Graphic Design.

title: Principles of Interaction Design
description: Examines user-centered principles for designing interactive systems, focusing on affordances, feedback, information architecture, and user flows.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interaction Design.

title: Interactive Prototypes
description: Teaches prototyping techniques for interactive products and services using wireframing, mockups, and user testing.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interaction Design.

title: Motion Design
description: Introduces time-based design using animation and motion graphics to communicate narratives and concepts across media.

title: Historic Interiors for Interior Design
description: The study of building interiors in relation to design, culture, history, furniture and decorative elements.This course is a requirement for completion of the BFA in Interior Design. May be used to satisfy an ARTH elective for the BA in Art History.

title: Principles of Interior Design
description: Introduces foundational interior design principles, including space planning, design research, conceptual development, and human-centered design. Students develop skills in visual communication, design elements, and iterative project development using both analog and digital approaches.Previously offered as Introduction to Interior Design.

title: Human Factors in Interior Design
description: Explores human factors in interior environments, emphasizing ergonomic, anthropometric, and sensory considerations that support health, safety, comfort, and well-being. Students apply human-centered design principles, accessibility standards, and research methods to create functional and responsive interior spaces.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interior Design.

title: Light and Color
description: Studies the interrelationship of light and color in shaping spatial and perceptual experience. Emphasizes theories of illumination and color, their aesthetic and emotional effects, and their application in interior environments to support function, atmosphere, and human well-being.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interior Design.

title: Interiors and Dwelling
description: Explores residential design as both physical and cultural construct, examining how space, place, and habitation shape human experience. Through studio projects, students integrate social, cultural, and psychological considerations into the design of home environments, developing spatial solutions that balance conceptual intent, function, and materiality.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interior Design.

title: Introduction to Graphic Design
description: Beginner-level studio introducing methods of solving visual communication problems with an emphasis on conceptual development and the practical application of design fundamentals.This course does not fulfill a requirement for the BFA in Graphic Design. This course fulfills a 300-level studio art elective for the BA or BFA in Art, track in Studio Art or track in Art History, or Minor in Art.

title: Introduction to Visualization
description: Beginner-level studio introducing methods of solving visual communication problems with an emphasis on conceptual development and the practical application of design fundamentals.

title: Principles of Product Design
description: Introduces product design processes, from research and sketching through materials, ergonomics, and sustainability considerations.

title: Consumer Product Design
description: Develops consumer product concepts, addressing manufacturing, marketing, user needs, and environmental responsibility.

title: Professional Practices in Design
description: Engages students in developing resumes and professional tools, along with mock interview and experiences with visiting designers and guest lecturers.

title: Environmental Graphics
description: Exploration of wayfinding, exhibition, and placemaking where visual communication meets architectural context. Emphasis on clarity, orientation, and spatial storytelling.

title: Product Interfaces
description: Study of interfaces for connected devices and embedded systems, joining tactile interaction with screen-based feedback and system states.

title: Immersive Experiences
description: Investigation of spatial computing across AR/VR/MR. Attention to presence, embodiment, and narrative in three-dimensional interaction.

title: Service Design
description: Design of services across channels, translating research into blueprints that align front-stage experiences with back-stage operations.

title: Retail Experiences
description: Integration of brand, merchandising, and spatial strategies across physical and digital retail touchpoints.

title: Sustainable Design Systems
description: Strategies for circularity, lifecycle thinking, and regenerative approaches across communication, products, and interiors.

title: Data Visualization and Storytelling
description: Translation of complex data into accessible visual narratives across static and interactive media.

title: Design for Social Innovation
description: Human-centered approaches for community challenges, emphasizing equity, accessibility, and shared value.

title: Wearables
description: Body-centered design integrating textiles, electronics, ergonomics, and interaction for health, performance, and expression.

title: Visual Identity Systems
description: Explores the creation of cohesive visual identity systems, including logos, typography, color, and brand guidelines across media.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Graphic Design.

title: Publication Design
description: Explores editorial design and publication production, integrating typography, layout, grid systems, and narrative structure across print and digital formats.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Graphic Design.

title: Packaging Design
description: Examines structural and visual considerations in designing sustainable packaging for products, including materials, branding, and user experience.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Graphic Design.

title: Directed Projects in Graphic Design
description: Independent study under faculty guidance focused on advanced inquiry in graphic design.

title: Internship in Graphic Design
description: Supervised professional experience in a graphic design context.May not be used to fulfill minimum requirements for the BFA in Graphic Design.

title: Experience Architecture
description: Strategizes user experiences for complex interactive problems, organizing large-scale information systems, mapping multi-part user flows, and addressing data management.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interaction Design.

title: Emerging Interfaces
description: Experiments with emerging technologies including augmented reality, virtual reality, mixed reality, and physical computing.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interaction Design.

title: Directed Projects in Interaction Design
description: Independent study under faculty guidance focused on advanced inquiry in interaction design.

title: Internship in Interaction Design
description: Supervised professional experience in interaction design practice.May not be used to fulfill minimum requirements for the BFA in Interaction Design.

title: Commercial Environments
description: Examines the design of commercial interior environments, including workplace, retail, and hospitality spaces, with emphasis on programming, building codes, branding, and user experience.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interior Design.

title: Interiors and Nature
description: Explores biophilic and sustainable interior design, integrating natural elements, light, materials, and ecological systems. Students develop spatial solutions through studio projects and iterative design processes that balance aesthetic, functional, and environmental considerations while supporting occupant well-being and cultural context.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interior Design.

title: Interiors Construction
description: Introduces interior construction principles, materials, methods, assemblies, and detailing, with attention to life safety, accessibility, and building codes. Students develop skills in interpreting construction documents, coordinating with building systems, and integrating structural, regulatory, and environmental considerations to create functional and aesthetically coherent interior spaces.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interior Design.

title: Building Systems
description: Introduces mechanical, electrical, plumbing, fire protection, and lighting systems and their integration into interior environments. Students explore system requirements, coordination with design intent, and sustainable strategies to create functional, code-compliant interiors that support occupant comfort and well-being.This course is required for completion of the BFA track in Interior Design.

title: Construction Documents
description: Introduces professional construction documents, technical drawings, and detailing techniques using industry-standard software.
